coredata.dc:description: High-performance materials called nanocomposites have unique features. Nanocomposites are in high demand in plastic engineering, with an anticipated periodic growth rate of 25 %. Its potential is so great that it is useful in numerous operations, from packaging to biomedical operations. It is anticipated that similar equipment and other intriguing operations will be required. The operations of nanocomposites display new specialized and marketable potential for numerous electronics, automotive, aerospace, and biotechnology sectors because of their environmental friendliness. Some studies on water uptake have shown that both ZnO nanorods and polymer polyaniline (PANI) function to prevent up to 75 % of liquid from penetrating the paint layer. With 4 % MWCNT content, a polypyrrole-multiwalled carbon nanotube (PPy-MWCNT) nanocomposites sensor was developed that was most sensitive to NH3 gas. The produced material maintained 86.9 % of its starting specific capacitance. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite results show that the high charge storage retention is 91.45 %. This paper will explain the importance of using nanocomposites in industry applications where the paper decides on coating, sensors, and energy storage.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-performance materials called nanocomposites have unique features. Nanocomposites are in high demand in plastic engineering, with an anticipated periodic growth rate of 25 %. Its potential is so great that it is useful in numerous operations, from packaging to biomedical operations. It is anticipated that similar equipment and other intriguing operations will be required. The operations of nanocomposites display new specialized and marketable potential for numerous electronics, automotive, aerospace, and biotechnology sectors because of their environmental friendliness. Some studies on water uptake have shown that both ZnO nanorods and polymer polyaniline (PANI) function to prevent up to 75 % of liquid from penetrating the paint layer. With 4 % MWCNT content, a polypyrrole-multiwalled carbon nanotube (PPy-MWCNT) nanocomposites sensor was developed that was most sensitive to NH3 gas. The produced material maintained 86.9 % of its starting specific capacitance. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite results show that the high charge storage retention is 91.45 %. This paper will explain the importance of using nanocomposites in industry applications where the paper decides on coating, sensors, and energy storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocomposites are used in many industries, including automotive, electronics, packaging, paints, biomedicine, and coatings, and have recently gained much attention [1]. Furthermore, nanocomposites in coating applications are designed to provide attractive, smart surface coatings that focus on corrosion, antimicrobial, and glue applications. These compounds provide new and distinctive features like increased strength, heat resistance, thermal stability, and mechanical stability [2]. Nanocomposite coatings are becoming cheaper, more effective, and more accessible [3]. Electrodeposition is the simplest method for fabricating anti-corrosive coatings and nanomaterials such as nanocontainer [3]. These materials were added to the composition to prevent active substances from encountering the coating matrix, which is "easy to clean" [4]. Coatings or self-cleaning are used to reduce water and energy consumption in cleaning facilities. In contrast, smart coatings are used to construct devices for solar photovoltaic, solar thermal, and solar-to-fuel conversion [5].

Nanocomposite sensors are used to develop sensitive and responsive sensors accurately to measure various chemical, physical, and biological variables [6]. For example, graphene oxide, polymers, and zirconium oxide can be sensors for gases such as ammonia or hydrogen gas. Nanocomposite energy storages improve efficiency, energy generation, device use, and storage [7]. For example, copper oxide and cadmium sulfide can be utilized in solar cells as active materials to convert light into electricity. Some materials are used in batteries to store and release energy. Nanocomposites, a molecular-level nanomaterial, is a pioneering advancement in coatings, sensors, and energy storage [8–10]. They offer durability, resistance to environmental factors, and functional versatility, protecting surfaces from ravages of time and use. Nanocomposites also contribute to heightened sensitivity and selectivity in sensors, enabling the detection of substances at previously undetectable levels. This is crucial for environmental monitoring and medical diagnostics [11]. Nanocomposites overcome traditional materials' limitations in energy storage by creating batteries and supercapacitors with higher capacities, faster charge-discharge rates, and longer life cycles [12]. This is essential for sustainable energy solutions, where efficient storage is as critical as the energy source [13]. Integrating nanocomposites into these applications is an improvement and a transformation that paves the way for advancements once thought to be science fiction. As we continue exploring these materials' potential, we unlock the door to a future where the possibilities are as vast as the nano-horizons themselves [14].

The current work uses different review techniques and definitions, types, preparation methods, and various characterization techniques to collect some reportable research that researchers have done regarding the use of nanocomposites in a range of applications, including coatings, sensors, and energy storage, and what enhancements have happened. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Raman spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), thermogravimetric analysis (TAG), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) are among the techniques being used to characterize these instruments. Nanocomposites play an important role in the development and innovation of many fields of technology and industry, opening new horizons for future applications and research.

Bibliometric analysis is a well-liked and accurate technique for discovering and analyzing vast amounts of scientific data. It enables us to disentangle the evolutionary distinctions of a given field, highlighting developed areas within that field. However, it has just recently been used in business research. Fig. 1 shows the number of research papers published by some countries during that period. As shown in Fig. 2, the names of the practical fields and the extent to which the articles published in them relate to each other in the content of the topic covered below. Fig. 3 is the network visualization of research related to the keywords of the research, Fig. 4 shows the types of articles, research, and research papers that were published on these topics, and Fig. 5 also shows the names of scientific topics that interested in studying the topics related to it.

Dhanapal et al. [15] prepared di-glycidyl ethers of bisphenol A (DGEBA) epoxy resin and betel nut fiber (AF) using a polyamidoamine curing agent, as shown in Fig. 6. The composites contained different weight percentages of nano-alumina with amine functionalization (F-Al) (1, 2, and 3 wt%). The prepared samples were inspected using XRD, AFM, SEM, and HRTEM methods. F-Al particles were introduced to have a synergistic effect and enhance the performance of care fibers in terms of moisture resistance.

Amer et al. [16] created a nanocomposite that was used as a coating system with hydrophobic characteristics and excellent anticorrosion properties. ZnO nanoparticles ranging from 1 % to 8 % (weight by weight) were added to a polymer acrylic-silicone hybrid matrix via a solution intercalation approach. All manufactured systems for nanocomposite coatings [16]. Felid emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), EDX, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), contact angle measurement (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques were used to investigate the morphology, wettability, and electrochemical properties. Silicone resin and acrylic resin have excellent crosslinking abilities. ZnO NPs can be added to coatings to improve their performance by making them more hydrophobic and improving their barrier abilities. A loading factor of 3 wt% in the hybrid polymer matrix led to optimal dispersion of the ZnO nanoparticles. On the other hand, the surface morphology results in excellent barrier performance, excellent anticorrosion ability, and dispersion nanoparticles of ZnO. The AS-3 system exhibits the most even distribution of ZnO nanoparticles. It demonstrated outstanding stability during all exposures according to the Nyquist plot in the capacitive loop and timings, as illustrated in Fig. 7.

Wang et al. [17] An examined nanocomposite of cobalt, phosphorus, and titanium (Co-P-TiO2) covering was created using an electrodeposition method. XRD was utilized to investigate the crystal structure, while AFM and SEM were used to characterize the surface. They successfully prepared and characterized coatings made of Co-P and Co-P-TiO2. Co-P-TiO2 coatings retain their amorphous structure even after the addition of TiO2 nanoparticles. The appropriate TiO2 content of 1 g/L formed a compact and smooth coating surface; beyond this point, TiO2 particle aggregation increased, and the coating surface became more open and irregular. Because of the TiO2 nanoparticles' effective dispersion, the 1 g/L TiO2-modified Co-P-TiO2 coating exhibits increased mechanical performance and corrosion resistance. By contrast, adding aggregated particles and undesirable surface microstructure of TiO2 gave worse results in coating performance. Oytun and Ozkose [18] proposed the amidation reaction between carboxylic acid-functionalized MWCNTs and amine-terminated PETOx (PEtOx-NH2) to produce multiwalled carbon nanotube/ polyoxazoline nanocomposites (MWCNT/PEtOx) with varying MWCNT contents. Spectroscopic, microscopic, and thermal investigations were used to characterize the morphological, structural, and thermal characteristics of the produced MWCNT/PEtOx nanocomposites. The results from TGA and DSC showed that all samples had increased thermal stability compared with poly (2-ethyl-2-oxazoline). Furthermore, MWCNT/PEtOx with dissimilar MWCNT loadings were sprayed over a glass substrate to create the nanocomposite films shown in Fig. 8. The nanocomposite films showed improved electrical conductivity and a well-distributed shape. This research led them to the conclusion that PEtOx/MWCNT nanocomposites can be used to create numerous applications that require conducting materials.

Seifzadeh, and Hollagh [19] used a homogeneous, pore-free electroless coating and compact Ni-Co-P and Ni-CoP-SiO2 nanocomposites via Ce-La-MnO4 (CLP) coating for conversion. These materials have been investigated to improve the corrosion resistance of the AZ91D magnesium alloy. SEM, XRD, EDX, and the coating microstructure were used to examine the prepared materials. When Ni-Co-P and Ni-P coatings are compared, it is found that Ni-Co-P coating has greater corrosion protection qualities and is more compact with a homogeneous structure. This improvement was significantly increased after the addition of SiO2 NPs and was most likely caused by the adsorption of SiO2 NPs on the surface of the alloy. Thick Ni-Co-P-SiO2 composite coatings showed improved resistance to corrosion compared with Ni-Co-P and Ni -P coatings because of the development of tortuous grain boundaries with compact coating. Ni-Co-P-SiO2 nanocomposites outperformed Ni Co-P and Ni-P electroless coatings in terms of anticorrosion performance because thick electroless coatings with tortuous nodular boundaries developed on them. Mostafaei and Nasirpouri [20] investigated the creation of a unique epoxy-based paint for use on carbon steel epoxy, which was primarily used in the paint composition, along with the electronically conductive polymer polyaniline (PANI), as well as a nanocomposite derivative of PANI that includes ZnO nanorods. SEM, XRD, UV–Vis, TEM, and FT-IR of the nanocomposite were performed to examine the product structure and phase. TEM showed that ZnO nanorods were implanted in the polymeric matrix. Studies on the water uptake by the produced coating panels showed that both ZnO and PANI nanorods function in the paint film as a p-n junction and can inhibit up to 75 % of liquid from penetrating the paint layer, extending the lifetime of the coating, as shown in Fig. 9. The use of conductive polymers like PANI-ZnO nanocomposite and PANI can lessen barnacles on the coated panels and the settlement of algae, according to antifouling and antibacterial research. In addition, up to 2 % of the ZnO nanorods significantly increase their antifouling capabilities. Among all paint formulas, coating EN 4.5-Z2 Table (2) in [20] demonstrated the best antifouling efficacy. The marine environment cannot leach the nanomaterials out of the coating because of their small size and the rod-like shape of zinc oxide. These improved epoxy nanocoatings can have greater fouling resistance. They can be used as environmentally friendly coatings on other marine structures or the hulls of ships.

Tibayan, et al. [21] investigated the coating performance of high-antibacterial-capacity Ag/SnO2 nanocomposites. The horizontal vapor phase growth (HVPG) approach generates Ag/SnO2 materials with high purity, a low creation cost, and an environmentally friendly synthesis. HVPG employs a thermal synthetic methodology to change the states of the materials through processing. Ag and SnO2 do really combine to generate nanorods of Ag and SnO2, according to analyses using SEM and DFT. The Alamar Blue Assay (ABA) absorbance method was used to examine the antibacterial qualities of these compounds. The findings showed that the amount of Ag in the composite impacted how toxic towards S. aureus and E. coli. They found that the ratio 1: 4 of Ag: SnO2 showed the most antibacterial activity. However, no color changes were observed in the Ag/SnO2 composites with nanoscale dimensions and high Ag/SnO2 ratios, indicating that these substances have excellent antibacterial properties. These findings imply that Ag/SnO2 nanocomposite materials are suitable for coatings with significant antibacterial activity and that nanoparticles have enhanced antibacterial properties. kamonwanon et al. [22] used four different types of composite mastic blocks for computer-aided manufacturing/computer-aided design (CAM/CAD) composite resin blocks (Avencia, Katana Lava Ultimate, Block HC, and Cerasmart) to determine the effects of SiO2-nanocomposite film coating on the hardness of the surface and the attachment of bacteria. Using CAM/CAD composites, a SiO2-nanocomposite film was applied to their surfaces, making them tougher and less conducive to the adhesion of S. mutans. The smoother and more hydrophobic surfaces of the coated specimens than those of the uncoated specimens may have contributed to the decreased bacterial adherence.

Abdollahi et al. [23] sed a 3-glycidoxypropyl-trimethoxysilane (GPTMS), tetra-ethoxy-silane (TEOS), and tetra-methoxy-silane (TMOS) as silane precursors in the preparation of organic-inorganic nanocomposite coatings for protection, along with AlOOH made from aluminum butoxide and TiO2 nanoparticles formed from tetra-n-butyl titanate. According to potentiodynamic scanning (PDS) and 2000-h salt-spray trials, the silane concentration, silane precursor type, and nanoparticle type affect how well hybrid coatings defend against erosion. On AA1050 substrates, dynamic light scattering (DLS) revealed the production of a homogenous, crack-free, uniform, and excellent protective coating with strong adhesion. The produced TiO2 particles were uniform with diameters between 40 and 50 nm, and AlOOH particles were also uniform with diameters between 50 and 60 nm. By switching from silane precursors made of TEOS to those made of TMOS for approximately 10 years, the protective impact of the coating was improved. Moreover, introducing TiO2 in the GPTMS-based coatings improved polarization resistance compared with AlOOH nanoparticles. TiO2 and AlOOH nanoparticles were added at the same time to provide strong protective coatings. The amount of inorganic material in GPTMS-based coatings affects the hydrophilicity of the surface.

Elbasuney and Maraden [24] developed an epoxy nanocomposite that was founded on the synergy between an improved intumescent flame retardant agent (also known as AP750 in the marketplace) and hydroxyapatite (HA) particles. This innovative nanocomposite showed improved flammability performance in cone calorimetry with a 51 % reduction in the peak heat generated, self-extinguishing, and overcoming the direct flame feed at 1700 °C. The coating’s mechanical barrier and corrosion resistance capabilities performed better. The temperature of the steel substrate was constantly tracked while a heat source was applied to the coated steel side at 900 °C. Pure epoxy showed a sharp rise in the heat release rate, whereas epoxy/AP750 showed an ignition effect by forming a weak extended charr layer. The creation of a thick, cohesive charr layer was evidence of the intumescent impact of the nanocomposite (HA/AP750/epoxy). Cone calorimeter testing of the prepared composite revealed a cohesive and integrated charr layer; as a result, it was used as an intumescent coating. The intumescent coating successfully shielded the substrate from the heat source, and the temperature reached equilibrium at 400 °C after 500 s. Alam et al. [25] A novel hydrophobic nanocomposite resist corrosion coating material based on Canola oil was created using different fume silica (FS) concentrations, as shown in Fig. 10. A straightforward technique was used to turn vegetable oil into a nanocomposite to fight corrosion.

DTA / TGA NMR, FTIR, contact angle, DSC, and SEM were employed to evaluate the thermal stability, morphology, and the nanocomposite's structure. Testing methods used to assess the effectiveness of the nanocomposite coating included electrochemical and physics-mechanical testing. In terms of their physical and mechanical properties, such as bend test, gloss, crosshatch, pencil hardness, impact resistance, and scratch hardness, the coatings significantly improved with the addition of FS. The greatest mechanical and physical properties (gloss, scratch resistance, impact, and bend) were found in the canola diol urethane-oxalate-amide polymer-based CAPUOA/FS nanocomposite. The results of this study suggest that anticorrosive coatings can be made from CAPUOA/FS nanocomposite coatings. Compared with the pure polymer, the efficiency of the nanocomposite coating was improved. The topic of hydrophobic anticorrosive coatings can be used in the manufactured nanocomposite coating.

Sebastian et al. [26] produced a nanocomposite combining epoxy resin and silica nanoparticles to create a mechanically strong anticorrosion super hydrophobic coating material. To examine super hydrophobia action for the synthesized material SEM, as shown in Fig. 11, goniometer, AFM, XRD, and potentiodynamic polarization measurements were measured. The coating's resistance to abrasion and mechanical toughness were investigated using abrasion tests, while mechanical characteristics, including decreased modulus, AFM, and Berkovich hardness, were assessed using nanoindentation testing. It was discovered to be abrasion- and corrosion-resistant, strong, and mechanically stable.

Chakradhar et al. [27] investigated how polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have been combined to create thermally stable super hydrophobic coatings. With increased MWCNT content, the transition from a hydrophobic to a super hydrophobic state was made possible. Employ XRD, FT-IR, FESEM, EDX, and XPS for sample characterization. It was discovered that the ideal concentration of MWCNT needed to create super hydrophobicity is 33 %. The coatings remain extremely hydrophobic even after heating to 573 K. The PVDF in the PVDF/MWCNT coating decays at 623 K, leading the coating to lose fluorine and to transition from a super hydrophobic to a super-hydrophilic state, according to EDS and XPS studies. Bahadur, and Sunkara [28] studied polyphenylene sulfide behavior (PPS) containing inorganic nanoparticles. ZnO, SiC, TiO2, and CuO were the fillers studied, and their particle sizes ranged from 30 to 50 nm. Compression molding was used to combine these fillers in various ratios to create polymer composites. Using optical microscopy and XPS, the adhesion between the transfer films and counter faces was evaluated according to the strength of the peel after examining the transfer films of the composite substances produced on the counter faces during sliding. The resistance to wear of PPS was demonstrated to be increased when it was filled with different amounts of TiO2 and CuO but reduced when filled with ZnO and SiC. The greatest resistance to wear was attained with 2 % filler. Tavangar et al. [29] reported that ceramic implants made from hydroxyapatite (HA) are ideal for orthopedic purposes. Production of HA-barium titanate (BT) composite samples by non-pressure sintering and mechanical milling. Commercial HA and BT produced by sol-gel synthesis created the composite. By sintering and cold isostatic pressing, composite scaffolds of HA-BT were created to augment pure HA's mechanical, physical, biological, and electrical properties. FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and SEM were used to investigate the crystal phases, molecular structure, and microstructure. They measured the bulk density values using the Archimedes method. The results showed that when they compared HA-BT scaffolds and pure HA scaffolds were compared, HA-BT had greater toughness, compressive strength, hardness, and density. After immersion in the simulated bodily fluid (SBF) solution, the HA-BT scaffold revealed more apatite deposits, giving it greater surface roughness than pure HA. HA improved electrical properties when BT was present. According to cell culture experiments, composites with 40, 50, and 60 wt percentages of BT had a high degree of biocompatibility, whereas the material containing 50 % weight of BT generated the best results. When BT was present as a secondary phase, the composite samples’ mechanical properties and bulk density improved, notably the 60-weight% BT and 40-weight% HA composites. All samples were biocompatible, according to the results of the biocompatibility testing, and there was no discernible difference in terms of cell proliferation between pure HA and composite scaffolds. Al-Tayyar. et al. [30] studied the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), Chitosan (CS), and nanocomposites made of SiO2 nanoparticles doped with ZnO nanoparticles to create antibacterial bio nanocomposite films (ZnO-SiO2). To create the PVA/CS/ZnO-SiO2 bio-nanocomposites, the ZnO -SiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized as shown in Fig. 12 and added at various ratios (0.50, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 %). These bio-nanocomposite films made of PVA, CS, ZnO, and SiO2 exhibit antibacterial activity and may reduce the negative environmental impact of current packaging materials. They manufactured ZnO-SiO2 nanocomposites and the PVA/CS/ZnO-SiO2 bio nanocomposite films. The properties of mechanical, the permeability to gasses, and water vapor transmission rate of the synthesized PVA/CS/ZnO-SiO2 bio nanocomposite films were evaluated. The created bio nanocomposite films showed better antibacterial activity against both G+ and G- bacteria, including S. aureus, S33R and (Escherichia coli, IRAQ 3). In line with this, we employed the prepared nanocomposite for bread packaging. The developed packaging substance, including 5 % ZnO-SiO2 nanocomposite, inhibited mold formation on the surface of the bread, corresponding to the colony forming unit method, and showed the best antibacterial activity against G-bacteria like E. coli and G+ positive bacteria like S. aureus. The obtained results showed that the bread packed in the prepared bio nanocomposite had a significantly improved visual appearance. These multiple observations and testing methods were combined to provide packaged bread with a longer shelf life and lower levels of food-borne pathogens. To decrease the negative influences of packing on the atmosphere.

Arjun et al. [31] created self-powered and flexible piezoelectric sensors by sandwiching polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanocomposite films. Nanofillers such as titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, and silica were chosen to create sensor films of the PVDF composite. The nanocomposites were subjected to several experiments to determine their thermal, electrical, and mechanical characteristics. The sensor characterization findings demonstrate that the inclusion of nanoparticles in the piezoelectric performance is enhanced by the PVDF matrix, with a maximum observed sensitivity of 103 mV/N for devices incorporating zinc oxide. Similar increases in sensitivity were also observed in sensors made with nano titanium dioxide particles. The polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) packaging extends support and shields the device from changes in ambient humidity. PDMS coating prolongs the life of sensors by preventing PVDF composite layer delamination. The findings of the sensor characterization demonstrate that PVDF-ZnO-based sensors have the highest degree of sensitivity, followed by PVDF-SiO2 and PVDF-TiO2, as illustrated in Fig. 13. To efficiently use the broad area sensing components of prosthetic devices, a simple, affordable sensor was manufactured, described, and used. Future work will likely involve creating an integrated transduction mechanism employing PVDF composites to fit into the smaller components of the prosthetic construction. Self-powered and flexible touch sensors were created using low-cost modification techniques, and they are intended for use in the big area components of prosthetic systems.

Rana et al. [32] Developed rGO-MoS2 nanocomposites by practical and economical microwave irradiation treatment. They were used to detect hydrazine by electrochemical method, as shown in Fig. 14. rGo and MoS2 were used as the starting ingredients for nanocomposites. Powder field emission, Raman spectrum analysis, SEM, TEM, and XRD were used to analyze the component structure, shape, and content. The detection limit, sensitivity, and crystallinity of the rGO-MoS2 nanocomposites were improved by their well-defined shape and 2D planar structure. Cyclic voltammetry has been employed in sensing investigations. Using modified gold electrodes, the relationship between current and potential was investigated. It was discovered that the sensitivity for rGO-MoS2 was 89.89 μA μM−1cm−2, and the detection limit was 132 nM. This was attributed to the nanocomposite’s facile transfer of charges, increased specific surface area, and excellent conductivity.

Hasanpour et al. [33] co-detected carbamazepine and amitriptyline using voltammetric measurements, an easy system for sensing MoSe2 with the adjustment of screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) and rGO nanocomposite (MoSe2-rGO/SPCE) was created. Several techniques were used to examine the composition. And shape of the produced materials, including EDX, FE-SEM, XRD, and FTIR. Two voltammetric techniques were used to investigate the electrochemical process of oxidation of amitriptyline: linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Data showed that the MoSe2-rGO/SPCE had a high current sensitivity to amitriptyline. With respectable recovery rates, the prepared sample effectively detected amitriptyline and carbamazepine in actual samples. This work created a volumetrically sensitive and straightforward sensing technology based on the synthesized sample to detect carbamazepine and amitriptyline simultaneously utilizing an easy methodology. Paul and Philip [34] used an easy coprecipitation technique. A thin layer of Mn0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 nanocomposite was employed to cover the structure of an inter-digital capacitive (IDC). The prepared sample over the IDC structure as a sensing material of NH3 gas. To confirm the crystal form and surface morphology for the material under investigation, FT-IR, TEM, and XRD were employed to analyze the sample. The impedimetric technique was used to measure the characteristics sensing for gasses. Even when the gas concentrations are very low at 7 ppm, the produced sample structure offers a highly effective alternative gas sensor with a wide range of dynamics, high degree of selectivity, and sensitivity for NH3 gas. The sensor usually responds within a few seconds to adsorption and desorption. All these findings indicate that this system’s technical performance and low production costs could lead to the commercialization of NH3 gas sensor. Yang et al. [35] prepared NiO/Ti3C2Tx MXene nanocomposites made using an ultrasonic self-assembly technique as shown in Fig. 15. For NH3 detection, NiO NPs with an approximate radius of 60 nm were uniformly connected to Ti3C2TxMXene and injected through the interlamellar gap of this material. The outcomes show how well the prepared sensor performs compared with the sensitivity of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene. Additionally, the nanocomposite sensor shows excellent long-term stability and repeatability. These findings demonstrated the NiO/Ti3C2Tx MXene sensor’s usefulness in NH3 gas detection.

Al-Jawad et al. [36] studied chemically oxidatively polymerizing aniline monomers in the shape of emeraldine salts, and polyaniline (PANI) was created. Polyaniline-cadmium sulfide nanocomposites (PANI-CdS) were created by varying the CdS concentrations (10–50 wt%). UV-Vis, XRD, SEM, and AFM measurements with a spectroscope revealed uniform dispersal of the particles in the PANI matrix and their interconnectivity. Electrical measurements revealed that PANI-CdS had superior sensitivity to NO2 gas compared with pure PANI, and the optimum temperature was 200°C. The optical band gap was reduced with increasing CdS concentration. He et al. [37] For electrochemical glucose biosensing, glucose oxidase (GOD) must be loaded, Cu2+ and hemin were assembled to form Cu-hemin MOFs, a novel porous material. SEM, UV-Vis, XRD, FTIR, and BET were used to confirm the creation of the MOFs. The nanocomposites displayed a hollow cage structure resembling a ballflower with many mesopores, the ability to successfully load GOD molecules into the pores, and a high specific surface area. The electrochemical glucose sensor's efficiency was greatly enhanced using a small detection limit of 2.73 mM and a wide linear range from 36.0 mM to 9.10 mM. This improvement offers a workable design for biomolecules and a brand-new MOFs-based electrochemical sensing platform. Al-Hartomy et al. [38] used hybrid composites of polyaniline and graphene nano-platelets (PANI-GRNPs) created by in situ chemical oxidative polymerization with varying GRNP contents in the PANI matrix. FTIR spectroscopy and SEM were applied to examine polymer nanocomposites' characterization and structural morphology. SEM micrographs showed that GRNPs were distributed uniformly throughout the PANI matrix, and FTIR analyses showed that GRNPs and PANI interacted to create a charge-transfer complex. These composite materials may have several technical uses, such as conductometric sensors. Toluene and benzene gasses were examined using PANI-GRNP composites, which are more active components in gas sensor devices than pure PANI at room temperature. According to studies on the sensing characteristics of these composites, their sensitivity increases linearly with gas concentration. Measurements of absorption and desorption throughout an entire cycle demonstrate the flawless conductometric performance of the composites. Kaur et al. [39] collected data from researchers and discovered that due to the alluring physicochemical properties. Researchers are very interested in nanocomposites (GQDs) and carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) for use in sensor technology. Various synthetic techniques for CQDs and GQDs include Laser ablation, hydrothermal technique, ultrasonic vibration, oxidative cleavage, arc discharge, synthesis using a microwave, solvothermal technique, chemical oxidation, impregnation, carbonization from small molecules or polymers, and organic synthesis all occur gradually. The detailed presentation of CQDs/GQDs and pure quantum dots nanocomposites as sensors reveals significant domains involving the detection of polluting compounds. Although pollutant detection is the main purpose of carbon-based QDs, they also offer outstanding degrees of flexibility and integration for multi-analyte identification, which opens various biochemical sensing applications and exciting environmental possibilities. Zhang et al. [40] illustrated that single-layer graphene has a large surface area that can be used to create graphene-based gas sensors. The properties of this synthesized nanocomposite were investigated by measuring the resistance variations caused by blowing with NH3 and HNO3 gas.

Kaolin-graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposites were produced using a straightforward approach to employ rGO for gas sensing. Compared with thermally annealed multilayer GO films, the results indicated that the nanocomposite films had a greater sensitivity. These films may be useful in situations where a cheap material with a significant conductive surface area is required. Parmar et al. [41] made films of graphene/polyaniline (C-PANI) nanocomposite and polyaniline (PANI) as toluene (C6H5-CH3) gas sensors. Using the NMP solvent, PANI- N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and (C-PANI-NMP) solutions are created, and the films are subsequently annealed at 230 °C. The prepared materials are examined with SEM and FTIR. Under various temperatures (30, 50, and 100 °C), the sensing abilities of the films were studied for 100 ppm of toluene in the environment. Although PANI had a response, a longer sensor and recovery time, and repeatability, C-PANI had good sensing performance. Temperature affected both polymer samples' sensor responses; however, PANI films' response and recovery times behaved differently when operating at 50 °C.

Wang et al. [42] By using simple ultrasonication and gradient centrifugation, monodispersed surfactant-free MoS2 nanoparticles with diameters of less than 2 nm were created from bulk MoS2. These nanoparticles expose a sizable number of edge sites, which makes the electrocatalytic activity for the H2O2 reduction process fascinating. They altered the surface structure at the atomic level and uncovered additional catalytically active edge locations. This made it possible to create a MoS2 biosensor with a huge linear range of 5 orders of scale and an extraordinarily small finding limit of 2.5 nM. An effective glucose biosensor was also created, and this biosensor was successfully recorded. Research conducted in these domains is anticipated to have potential commercial applications. Wang et al. [43] considered single-layer MoS2-graphene nanosheet (SL-MoS2-GNS) mixtures were created by simultaneously reducing hydrazine in the existence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and (NH4)2MoS4 with graphene oxide sheets as given in Fig. 16. By using Raman spectroscopy, TERM, SEM, and XRD, the shape and the synthesized materials' structures were investigated. According to EIS, the GNS in the composite considerably enhanced the electron transport in the lithiation and de-lithiation processes and lowered the electrode's contact resistance. The composites functioned well as a Li-ion battery anode, providing a sizable ability to reverse and high cycle stability. The synthesis was made easy and scalable, and the composites' MoS2 sheets were single-layered and widely scattered, according to the characterization.

Zhang et al. [44] used Graphite and HAuCl4 as precursors in the hydrothermal synthesis of a reduced (rGO)/Au hybrid nanocomposite. Characterization reveals the production of rGO/Au, including XRD, Raman spectra, XPS, and TEM. When the prepared material detects NO2 at 50 °C, a gas sensor demonstrates increased sensitivity, quicker response-recovery process, and great repeatability. At a low working temperature, Au nanoparticle loading may greatly improve the NO2 detecting capabilities of graphene-based sensing materials, indicating outstanding prospective applications as gas sensors. Sahiner [45] created three-dimensional (3D) graphene oxides (GO), ultra-porous graphene aerogel (GA) were chemically reduced using L-ascorbic acid (L-AA) and then freeze dried. Composites made of GA/ poly pyrrole (PPy) and GA/ poly aniline (PANI) interpenetrating conductive polymers, such as PPy and PANI, were created within the super pores of the 3D GA prepared as GA/PPy and GA/PANI interpenetrating composites (IPC). Electrical conductivity was found to be reduced by 4000 times after 60 minutes of exposure to CO2 gas at ambient temperature and pressure, indicating the possibility of using the produced materials as CO2 gas sensors. The synthesized samples were characterized by using FTIR and TGA techniques.

Bachhav and Patil [46] studied the nanocomposite that was made using an in situ chemical oxidative polymerization method carboxylate multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and poly pyrrole (PPy) utilizing ammonium persulphate (APS) as an oxidant and HCl as a dopant. To achieve homogeneous dispersion inside the PPy matrix, the MWCNTs were carboxylic functionalized and ultrasonically processed. The PPy-MWCNT nanocomposite was characterized using FE-SEM, UV-Vis, XRD, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy. According to the results, the pelletized sensor described here was reliable, economical, and somewhat stable at room temperature, which also revealed that the sensitivity improved with a concentration of NH3. The PPy-MWCNT nanocomposites sensor that was most sensitive to NH3 gas was created with a 4-weight percent MWCNT content. All the previous data are collected in Table 1 that contains the different synthesized nanocomposites used in industry (coating and sensor).

The growing use of unsustainable energy sources like coal and oil has resulted in environmental problems. In recent years, energy storage devices have emerged as one of the hottest topics. Their applications rely on their intrinsic properties [49]. Current research focuses on boosting energy density and creating lighter and more flexible materials. Dielectric capacitors have a significant energy storage capacity. Nanostructured materials offer significantly higher electrical conductivity and ionic transfer than common batteries and supercapacitor substances. In addition, they enable complete intercalation site utilization in the particle volume, producing large specific capacities and quick diffusion of ions. These characteristics enable electrodes composed of nanomaterials to support significant currents, rendering them an efficient option for storing high energy [50]. Bishwakarma et al. [51] investigated a unique one-step electrochemical discharge technique (ECDP) that was utilized to create GO-ZnO nanocomposites (NCs) without the need for a catalyst or hazardous chemical reagent. Investigating using these ready materials as electrodes in energy storage devices like supercapacitors. The electrical efficiency of the produced GO-ZnO NCs was investigated. The analysis of the morphology of the NCs prepared under ideal conditions revealed multilayer graphene oxide coated NCs in the shape of flowers, which was supported by TEM and FESEM images. This work indicates a bright future for recycling e-waste into useful nanomaterials like ZnO and GO from old dry-cell batteries. This technique attempted to address environmental concerns and economic viability by employing these materials as potential applications for high-efficiency energy storage systems. Chakraborty et al. [52] modified the styrene-maleic anhydride (SMA) copolymer with a thiadiazole moiety and added greenly produced ZnO nanoparticles. The created material has much potential as a green replacement for current supercapacitors. Green-generated nanoparticles of ZnO were inserted into the matrix to improve the supercapacitor behavior. By utilizing readily available energy from the human body, this work integrates the ideas of energy storage and conservation ideas, which was extremely important for developing supercapacitors. The materials also have high-capacity retention and outstanding cycle stability. Çıplak et al. [53] created a ternary nanocomposite of reduced graphene oxide (rGO), gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), and polyaniline (PANI) using an inexpensive, two-step procedure that was environmentally friendly. The first step was reducing GO and stabilizing it using Cetraria islandica L. Ach lichen extract. Second, nanosheets of rGO were coated with Au@PANI. Au@PANI was synthesized by in-situ polymerization process for an aniline monomer with Chloroauric acid as oxidizing agent as shown in Fig. 17. The homogenous conductive gold nanoclusters inside the thin dodecyl benzene sulfonic acid (DBSA) added to the polymeric matrix coating rGO surface rendered it possible to produce conducting films made of nanocomposite and supply of short ion transfer channels. Using UV-Vis, FT-IR, XRD, SEM and HRTEM, the characteristics of prepared materials were examined. Pseudocapacitance and the electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC) techniques used for storing charge in the triple nanocomposite film showed significant rate performance, high specific capacitance, coulombic efficiency, and high stability during cycles. After five thousand cycles of continuous usage at two A g−1 electric current density, the produced material maintained 86.9 % of its starting specific capacitance.

Shaheen et al. [54] studied the chemical components from the plant E. cognata that were used to generate a ZnO-Co3O4 nanocomposite by using the sol-gel technique as shown in Fig. 18. The process of making ZnO-Co3O4 required annealing a metal complex formed from a phyto-organic framework in the air after treating the precursor solution with a plant organic complex. Additionally, they altered the surface chemistry of ZnO-Co3O4 using chemical molecules from E. cognata. The additional photo-stabilizing compounds based on carbon in the generated nanomaterial were first identified in this study. The morphology of the generated material was studied by investigating the organic functional groups linked to the created nanocomposites using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, TEM, FESEM, and EDX. Lastly, they employed XPS to investigate the surface chemistry of the nanomaterial produced via photosynthesis. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots revealed a sizeable capacitance at both high and low voltage scan rates and densities of the current. The predicted capacitance and low resistance were compared with several published research, which demonstrated the ZnO-Co3O4 produced from bioorganic frameworks had excellent potential as a supercapacitor.

Ashokkumar et al. [55] examined the effectiveness of copper oxide (CuO) and polyaniline (PANI) nanocomposites electrochemically, as shown in Fig. 19, for energy storage device applications utilizing the cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique and galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD) process. The produced nanocomposite was characteristics by SEM, which revealed that the surface morphology significantly changes with increasing CuO nano concentration to a tiny rod-like structure from the granular form. FT-IR peaks' shift to a larger number of waves with more CuO present confirmed the appropriate chemical interaction. The results of the TGA, DTA, and DSC tests indicated that the thermal stability of the synthesized material had improved. Particle sizes between 30 and 80 nm were shown by TEM, and a dense black patch imbedded on the surface of a white fiber background verified the existence of additional ions for metals in the prepared sample. When the amount of CuO nanocomposites was increased, the optical band gap decreased, and the UV-Vis confirms that the absorption wavelength exhibited a red shift. The findings suggested that the prepared sample is crucial for improving the long-lasting cycle stability of supercapacitors.

Chang et al. [56] Created the Pd/Fe2O3 nanoflakes nanocomposite using a straightforward redox reaction under hydrothermal conditions. The produced nanocomposite charge-storage process demonstrates that significant amounts of electrical charge were capacitively saved. The material’s XRD, FESEM, EDX, XPS, TEM, Raman spectra and BET characteristics were determined. The BET study revealed that compared with commercially available Fe2O3, the synthesized Pd/Fe2O3 nanoflakes had a large pore volume and surface area. Due to the results obtained from BET and the lower resistance for charge transfer, the generated Pd/Fe2O3 demonstrated superior specific capacitance in a strongly acidic electrolyte than commercial Fe2O3. Notably, using an acidic electrolyte, the developed Pd-Fe2O3 nano-flakes nanocomposite demonstrates extraordinary stability during over 8000 continuous charge-discharge cycles. After cycles, the nanocomposite's retention-specific capacitance was 87 %.

As a result, an artificial nanocomposite like Pd/Fe2O3 nanoflakes may one day serve as the electrode material for an energy storage device. Maksoud et al. [57] produced Cu/Cu2O NPs with a rod-like morphology outside their natural habitat using a simple and eco-friendly gamma radiation technique. Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) was mixed with Cu/Cu2O NPs at different weight ratios (0, 2.5, 5, and 7.5 %wt) to create PVC/Cu/Cu2O. SEM images further supported the Cu/Cu2O nanoparticles' rod-like shape, and XRD was measured. The optical band gap for PVC was 5.36 eV and then became 3.49 eV with increasing Cu/Cu2O concentration. As the frequency increased, the activation energy of the produced materials decreased. In general, prepared materials are ideal for use in the field of storage energy due to their improved dielectric and optical properties. Zhang et al. [58] created a nanocomposite film made of TiO2 and polyaniline (PANI) using the electrostatic spray deposition and electropolymerization techniques, as shown in Fig. 20. The nanocomposite film demonstrated outstanding electrochemical properties across both electrochromic and storage energy areas. The prepared sample was characterized using XRD, TEM, FESEM, and FT-IR. The color change performance was unaffected by the vast area and patterned programable device setup. This led to the design and construction of a multicolor TiO2/PANI composite-based intelligent energy-storage indicator with energy level monitoring capabilities. The many color changes made the real-time visual identification of the energy-storage level possible. The method developed in this work for creating multicolored nanoparticles can be scaled up for use in bifunctional electrochromic energy storage.

Payami and Mofrad [59] produced in a simple physical way by combining conductive polymers like PANI and Fe3O4 with plus redox active ferrocenyl modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to generate novel types of ferrocenyl-based NP electrodes to produce powerful supercapacitors as shown in Fig. 21. The resulting electrode materials have impressive charge storage and transport characteristics because of the integration of well-known redox-active organometallic compounds such as ferrocene, PANI, and Fe3O4 as basic materials. EDX, Raman spectroscopy, XRD, FT-IR, FE-SEM, and H NMR were all used to evaluate all synthesized nanomaterials. The electrochemical characteristics of the produced nanomaterials were Primarily evaluated using acceptable methods in a three-electrode setup.

These techniques were electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), as illustrated in Fig. 21. A total of 5000 cycles were used to evaluate the cycle efficiency at 1 A g−1 of current density. Outstanding storage of charge capacity of 91.45 percent using GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI as electrode material in future energy storage technologies was established.

Yi et al. [60] investigated the synthesized BaxSr1-xTiO3@ Dopamine (DA) and ferroelectric polymer polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)/ BaxSr1-xTiO3@DA nanocomposites with various filler compositions. They used oxalate to create barium strontium titanate nanoparticles with various compositions. BaxSr1-xTiO3 nanoparticles were covered with polydopamine due to dopamine self-polymerization. Semi-crystalline PVDF was used as the polymer matrix to create a variety of PVDF/BaxSr1-xTiO3 @DA nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 22. The interface polarization of the composite material will be further improved by adding ceramic powder with a high dielectric constant, increasing the material's dielectric constant at low frequencies. Ferroelectric phase nanofillers display a higher dielectric loss than nanocomposites loaded with pyroelectric phase fillers. XRD, FTIR, SEM, and TEM techniques were performed to examine the prepared material. The largest discharge energy density equals 7.8 J/cm3, and the greatest discharge efficiency of energy is reached at 65.0 % at 300 kV/mm. Both were achieved by the prepared material with a nanofiller concentration of 1 vol%.

Using a straightforward co-precipitation technique, Xu et al. [61] generated nanocomposites of rod-shaped MnO2 and carbon nanotubes treated with acid (a-CNT). On the surface of CNTs that have undergone acid treatment, MnO2 nanorods were produced inside by a redox mechanism involving KMnO4 and Mn(CH3COO)24H2O. In-depth research has been performed on the morphology, chemistry, and crystalline structure of MnO2 /CNT nanocomposites as they have been created. XRD, HRTEM, SEM, and FT-IR were used to examine the prepared materials. Studies have suggested that the produced samples' electron transfer efficiency was significantly increased, producing high electrical conductivity. According to research, the electron transfer performance of the synthesized samples was significantly better, leading to high electrical conductivity. At a variety of current densities, the composites display remarkable zinc ion storage capacity and reversibility. The produced samples could be used as cathode material because of their high efficiency towards recharging Zn- ion batteries. Wu et al. [62] produced iron chloride as an oxidant, thiophene as a reagent, and chloroform as a solvent during in situ chemical oxidative polymerization, and unique sulfur/polythiophene (S-PTh) core/shell composites were produced. To increase the effectiveness of the electrochemical process and the capacity of Li-S batteries, they investigated several S-PTh composite ratios. The produced materials were analyzed using electrochemical methods, FT-IR, TEM, XRD, elemental analysis, and SEM. The S-PTh electrode's starting discharge efficiency reached 1119 mA h g−1, but when 80 cycles were completed, the value of the residual efficiency reached 830 mA h g−1. In a Li-S battery, the prepared material as an electrode significantly surpassed the pure sulfide electrode in the measurements of rate performance, sulfur applications, and cycle life.

Sahoo et al. [63] created rGO, NiMn2O4, and polyaniline (GNMOP) using the hydrothermal technique shown in Fig. 23. FESEM, HRTEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR were used to assess the structure and shape of the prepared material. GNMOP was porous, as determined by BET measurements. The given capacitance reached 757 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 recent density using GNMOP, which was significantly greater than the value from the nanocomposite consisting of two materials and transition metal oxide merged. Additionally, GNMOP achieved an outstanding capacitance of 93 percent after two thousand cycles, and the maximum energy density was 70 Wh kg−1. In summary, this synthetic method can synthesize porous nanostructured tertiary nanohybrids. This study might also open the door to applying cutting-edge hybrid electrode components in supercapacitor applications.

Mondal et al. [64] produced quantum dots of graphene -doped polyaniline composites (GQDP). Making advanced materials for supercapacitor electrodes was the objective of this work by taking advantage of any potential synergy between PANI and GQD, as shown in Fig. 24. They used SEM, TEM, and a UV lamp to characterize the resultant nanocomposite. Overall, they successfully converted graphene oxide flakes into graphene quantum dots that were approximately 6.0 nm in size. The resulting GQDP composites have either a nanotubular or nanofibrous structure. The GQD influenced the inner diameter of GQDP nanotubes to aniline weight ratio (w/w). The synthesized GQDP composites, after 3000 cycles, exhibit an 80.1 percentage retention of capacitance and a maximum certain capacitance at one A g−1 current density reached 1044 Fg−1.

Wang et al. [65] produced a tertiary electrode substance for supercapacitors using graphene, SnO2, and poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene, or PEDOT), which were all produced in a single pot. The finished tertiary nanocomposite is an exciting electrode material that offers considerable energy, power, and excellent recyclability because of the synergetic effect of the three materials. XRD, TEM, SEM, and XPS measurements were performed on the prepared material. The capacitance storage with one A g−1 current density measured after five thousand cycles was approximately 100 % in the case of H2SO4 and 70 % by using Na2SO4. The findings of this study indicate that a single category of fascinating possibilities for future usage in energy storage or power generation technologies is a tertiary compound with such improved electrochemical stability inside an acidic electrolyte. All the previous data are collected in Table 2, which contains the different synthesized nanocomposites used in Storage devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and opportunities of nanocomposite in industry applications
ce:section.ce:para:
Nanocomposite materials comprise nanoscale fillers and polymer matrices such as nanoparticles, nanotubes, nanofibers, or graphene. These materials have received much attention because they can display unique characteristics and functionalities that traditional composites cannot. Nanocomposite materials have advantages such as increased electrical conductivity, thermal stability, mechanical strength, optical transparency, and barrier characteristics. However, synthesizing and applying nanocomposite materials also involves some challenges. Some examples of these challenges are:
•
Controlling the distribution and organization of nanofillers inside the polymer matrix impacts the interactions between the components and the composite's resultant properties. However, obtaining a uniform and ordered dispersion of nanofillers is difficult due to their high surface energy, inclination to agglomerate, and compatibility concerns with the polymer matrix.
•
Scaling up the manufacturing of nano-fillers and nanocomposites. Bottom-up procedures, such as chemical vapor deposition, sol-gel, or hydrothermal processes, are frequently used to create nanofillers. These methods are time-consuming, energy-intensive, and require precise control of the reaction parameters. Nanocomposites are typically created by combining nanofillers with polymer solutions or melts that may contain significant shear pressures, high temperatures, or hazardous solvents. Because of their high cost, low efficiency, and environmental effects, these technologies are not appropriate for large-scale industrial applications.
•
Achieving uniform dispersion and compatibility of nanofillers with polymer matrices can prevent aggregation and phase separation, which can limit composite performance.
•
Characterization and modeling of the properties and behaviors of nanocomposite materials. Nanocomposite materials exhibit complex and nonlinear phenomena that are affected by a variety of factors, including the type, shape, size, concentration, surface chemistry, distribution of nanofillers, crosslinking of the polymer matrix, and interfacial interactions between nanofillers and matrix. Therefore, measuring and predicting the properties and behaviors of nanocomposite materials using traditional techniques and models for bulk materials is difficult.
•
Understanding the kinetics and thermodynamics of nanomaterial and polymer assemblies can influence the production of ordered structures and emergent features in nanocomposites.
•
Ensuring the sustainability and safety of nanocomposite materials may require considering nanofillers' potential dangers and negative effects on the environment and human health.
•
Achieving a low limit of detection (LOD) allows the measurement of the target analyte at incredibly low quantities.
•
Suppressing the non-specific adsorption of interfering species prevents the unwanted attachment of other molecules that can impair sensor function.
•
Maintaining the sensor's reproducibility and stability in complex actual matrices means producing reliable and consistent data in various situations and conditions.
•
Achieving high energy and power densities or the capacity to store and distribute significant energy in a very small space and in a short time.
•
Enhancing the energy storage device's capacity to work after numerous cycles of charge and discharge while increasing cycling stability and durability.
•
Reducing costs and environmental effects requires using abundant, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly materials and manufacturing techniques to create energy storage devices.
•
Improving the energy storage device's safety and dependability entails reducing or eliminating the hazards of fire, explosion, leakage, or degradation under various operating circumstances.
Nanocomposite materials are a new type of polymeric material in which at least one component has nanoscale dimensions. They have many potential applications in various industries, including automotive, environmental, energy, and biomedical, and have many opportunities in energy storage, coating, and sensors because they can improve the performance, stability, and functionality of these devices, as well as the properties and functionality of the coatings. Some of the opportunities for nanocomposite materials in many fields are as follows:
•
Because of their superior thermal, mechanical, and processing qualities, they can replace metals in applications such as automotive and other industries.
•
By employing graphene as a nanofiller with remarkable electrical and optical properties, they can improve the performance of solar energy conversion and electrochemical energy devices.
•
By regulating the distribution and ordering of nanofillers inside the polymer matrix, they can add novel functionality unavailable in random composites.
•
They may improve environmental protection and nanomaterial recovery by reducing aggregation and loss during reaction processes.
•
Nanocomposite materials can improve the efficiency and capacity of batteries and supercapacitors by using graphene as a conductive and flexible substrate for metal oxide nanoparticles. Graphene can also prevent metal oxide aggregation and dissolution, reducing the electrodes' cycling performance.
•
Using metal oxide nanoparticles as active sensing elements on graphene sheets and nanocomposite materials can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of gas sensors. Metal oxide nanoparticles can interact with gas molecules and affect their electrical resistance, whereas graphene sheets can enhance the signal and offer a quick reaction.
•
Nanocomposite materials can offer novel applications in thermoelectric devices using metal oxide nanowires embedded in graphene films. Metal oxide nanowires have a high Seebeck coefficient and low thermal conductivity, whereas graphene films have high electrical conductivity and mechanical support.
•
Nanocomposite coatings can improve metal corrosion and wear resistance by using nanoparticles as fillers in the polymer matrix. These nanoparticles can reduce porosity, zigzag the diffusion channel for harmful substances, and provide excellent hardness and thermal stability.
•
Nanocomposite coatings can achieve superhydrophobic and self-cleaning surfaces using graphene or carbon nanotubes as fillers in the polymer matrix. These nanoparticles can produce a rough, hydrophobic surface that repels water and debris and provides electrical and optical properties.

In conclusion, these challenges and opportunities necessitate additional research and development to overcome limitations and optimize the performance of nanocomposite materials. Nanocomposites have the potential to revolutionize a wide range of scientific and technological disciplines, including industry, energy storage, sensors, coating, and environmental engineering. Therefore, solving these issues and investigating novel opportunities for developing ordered nanocomposite materials with innovative functions is critical.

Scientists have achieved some amazing advancements in nanotechnology. Researchers have worked on nanomaterials to improve their qualities in addition to other field applications. We have collected credible research on using nanocomposites and their potential applications for this study. Many forms of nanocomposites with improved qualities are employed in coating, sensors, and storage applications. The mechanical, electrical, optical, and magnetic characteristics of individual bulk or nanomaterials can be improved by the formation of the nanocomposites, as well as their chemical and corrosion resistance. Moreover, it can make materials more useful and stable as flows:
•
These results show that nanocomposite materials are appropriate for coating applications with significant antibacterial activity, increased mechanical performance, and corrosion resistance. According to the results of the biocompatibility testing using HA-barium titanate (BT) composite, the mechanical characteristics improved after adding up to 60 wt% BT. Studies on water uptake showed that both ZnO nanorods and PANI function to prevent up to 75 % of liquid from penetrating the paint layer, extending the lifetime of the coating.
•
Flexible and self-powered touch sensors were created using low-cost fabrication techniques, and they are intended for use in large-area components of prosthetic systems. All these findings indicate that this system's technical performance and low production costs could lead to the commercialization of NH3 gas sensors, conductometric sensors for determining the presence of toluene at low concentrations, and sensors with sensitivity for NO2 gas. According to the result, the PPy-MWCNT nanocomposite sensor that was most sensitive to NH3 gas was created with a 4 % MWCNT content.
•
Nanocomposites can be used as medical implants or energy-storage systems inside the body that could be powered by body cells or power generation technologies in the future. They are tertiary compound with improved electrochemical stability inside an acidic electrolyte. By using a composite consisting of rGO after five thousand cycles of continuous usage at two A g-1 electric current densities, the produced material maintained 86.9 % of its starting specific capacitance. The GQD composite result showed a high charge storage retention of 91.45 % as an electrode material in future energy storage technologies. In some composites, the largest discharge energy density equaled 7.8 J/cm3, and the greatest discharge energy efficiency reached 65 % at 300 kV/mm, both achieved by the prepared material with a nanofiller concentration of 1 vol%.
